channel.title: Oil on The Tradewinds Brief
channel.description: Recent content in Oil on The Tradewinds Brief

item.title: Uncle Ramesh: Exxon Investing in We Future, Ali Preparing We for Global Storm, and PPP Government Delivering Despite Opposition Noise
item.description:
Morning to all my people. Uncle Ramesh here, cup of Mauby in hand, looking out over the backdam and thinking about what the papers saying this Sunday. Let me walk you through it the way it really is, not the way the opposition vexers trying to paint it.

1. Ram and his “amateurish” talk

So Christopher Ram go on radio and call the government “amateurish” for not ring-fencing the Stabroek Block. You know what Uncle Ramesh think? Easy to talk big when you not the one at the table with Exxon.

item.title: Sunday Brief: Exxon Keeps $23.6 Billion While We Get the Leftovers, Ali Tells Us to 'Brace' for More Pain, and Apparently the Strait of Hormuz Is Now Our Problem
item.description:
Good Sunday morning, Guyana. The papers this weekend read like a collective exhale, and not the good kind. The kind where you realize you have been holding your breath for four years and the air finally comes out sounding like a tire deflating on the East Bank Highway. Pour your coffee. Let us walk through it.

1. The ExxonMobil arithmetic that refuses to go away

Kaieteur ran the numbers again and they still do not add up in our favour. Between 2020 and 2024, ExxonMobil, Hess, and CNOOC — the Stabroek Block partners — pulled in US$29 billion in profits. Guyana, the sovereign nation on whose seabed this oil is sitting, received US$5.4 billion in the same period. This is our “50/50 partnership.” The math is, as dem boys would say, mathing in a particular direction.

item.title: The Indo-Caribbean Brief: How India Became One of Guyana's Most Important Strategic Partners
item.description:
The Indo-Caribbean Brief: How India Became One of Guyana’s Most Important Strategic Partners

The Georgetown Ledger goes beyond the headlines. How Guyana actually works — with the receipts.

Five years ago, Guyana was a small South American country with a large diaspora and a modest economy. Today, it is one of the fastest-growing oil producers in the world. That transformation did not just change Guyana’s balance sheet. It changed who pays attention to it.

item.title: Daily Brief — Friday, April 10, 2026
item.description: Oil revenues surge past $159B in Q1, Ali eyes Gulf storage deals, police graduate 101 new recruits, and the digital traffic registry is coming whether you want it or not.

item.title: Patriots Portfolio — April 10, 2026
item.description: This week's Patriots Portfolio: Q1 oil revenue at US$761M, the SME Development Bank's US$100M funding window, Guyana's oil production approaching 1M barrels per day, and what the Palmyra highway means for Berbice investment.

item.title: Ramesh Sees It Differently — April 10, 2026
item.description: G$159 billion in Q1, Gulf investment talks, digital road safety, and new police — Ramesh explains why each development confirms what he has always believed about this administration.

item.title: Guyana Daily Brief – Thursday, April 9, 2026
item.description: Your 5-minute briefing on everything happening in Guyana today — cash grants, river wars, and an American banker who flew in to see what all the fuss is about.

item.title: Uncle Ramesh Sees It Differently – Thursday, April 9, 2026
item.description: Uncle Ramesh reviews the week's news and finds the government, as usual, doing everything right despite what the papers say.

item.title: Guyana Daily Brief – Wednesday, April 8, 2026
item.description: Guyana pockets US$761M in oil revenue, denies secret payout to gas contractor, and a cop on video says 'I'll kill you.' Wednesday, April 8.

item.title: Ramesh Sees It Differently – April 8, 2026
item.description: Ramesh on the US$761M oil windfall, the St Kitts agreements, the Wales project, Suriname's river fees, and why the press keeps missing the point.

item.title: Weekly Progress Report — April 8, 2026
item.description: This week: US$761M in Q1 oil revenue, the Wales GTE project director linked to Venezuela corruption, GYEITI civil society standoff, 22,000 street lights installed, and a $604M highway begins.

item.title: Patriots Portfolio – Friday, April 3, 2026
item.description: This week's Patriots Portfolio — what the 38% Trump tariff means for Guyana's economy, the Wales Gas Plant arbitration fallout, Exxon's Canje exit, and what patriotic investors need to watch.

item.title: Progress Report – Wednesday, April 1, 2026
item.description: A frank assessment of where things stand — what's moving, what's stalled, and what was announced with great fanfare and then quietly forgotten.

item.title: Uncle Ramesh – Wednesday, April 1, 2026
item.description: Uncle Ramesh is proud. Very proud. The government is doing everything right, and the opposition is just confused.

item.title: Daily Brief – Wednesday, April 1, 2026
item.description: Your Wednesday morning roundup of Guyana's top stories — flooding warnings, digital IDs, oil drama, and the AFC demanding government fork over $9 billion.

item.title: Daily Brief — Tuesday, March 31, 2026
item.description: Oil hits 918K bpd, Georgetown floods, GUYOIL raises fuel prices, a teen is stabbed in Lacytown, and Uncle Sam wants his tax treaty. Your Tuesday briefing.

item.title: Uncle Ramesh's Take — Tuesday, March 31, 2026
item.description: Uncle Ramesh is proud of the oil numbers, patient about the flooding, and completely unbothered by people complaining about the fuel price increase.

item.title: Guyana Daily Brief — Friday, March 27, 2026
item.description: Government grabs 22 Georgetown streets, Exxon builds an 8th oil ship without approval, and Kristi Noem visits State House. Plus: a rubber duck, a stolen $7M, and Uncle Ramesh has thoughts.

item.title: Patriots Portfolio — March 27, 2026: Streets, Ships, and Sovereignty
item.description: This week: Exxon starts building Oil Ship No. 8 without government approval, the government takes 22 Georgetown streets, and Guyana keeps printing economic records. The Patriots Portfolio tracks the business of Guyana.

item.title: Uncle Ramesh — Friday, March 27, 2026
item.description: Uncle Ramesh reads the Chronicle and sees nothing but progress. The streets, the oil, the cash grant — all going according to plan. As usual.

item.title: Patriots Portfolio — March 24, 2026: Oil, Courts, and the Business of Everything
item.description: This week in Guyana's business and economic landscape: Exxon's production push, rising oil prices, the NRF, and what the Mohameds saga means for business confidence.

item.title: Patriots Portfolio — Week of March 19, 2026
item.description: Your weekly satirical investment guide to the Guyanese national situation. Buy, Sell, or Hold.

item.title: Uncle Ramesh's Corner — Wednesday, March 19, 2026
item.description: Uncle Ramesh reads only the Chronicle, sees only the bright side, and adds what the Brief missed.

item.title: Wednesday Brief: Oil Hits $100, Mohameds Rejected Again, and Guyana's Last Independent Paper is Gone
item.description: Oil hits triple digits, the Court of Appeal delivers another blow to the Mohameds, Guyana's cash grant starts flowing to people who already have bank accounts, and we say goodbye to Stabroek News.

item.title: 📊 Patriots Portfolio – Friday, February 13, 2026
item.description: Your weekly economic snapshot. Budget 2026 allocations rolling in. Oil production steady. Stabroek News closure signals media market shift.

item.title: Monday Brief: Auditor General Goes Silent While Billions Vanish, 77 Cubans Screened for Trafficking, and Hetmyer Breaks a World Cup Record
item.description: Christopher Ram questions whether the Auditor General is actually auditing anything, 77 Cubans are screened for suspected human trafficking, more than 20 homes bulldozed in Circuitville, and Hetmyer smashes the fastest WI fifty in T20 World Cup history.

item.title: Speedeet & Wilar: The Biggest Ship in the Sea
item.description: Speedeet and Wilar visit the waterfront and learn about the giant ships pumping Guyana's oil — and ask the questions that matter.

item.title: Patriots Portfolio: Budget 2026 Deep Dive — Where Is the $1.588 Trillion Actually Going?
item.description: This week’s Patriots Portfolio breaks down the biggest budget stories: the $1.588 trillion allocation, tourism boom, gold sector resurrection, cash grants debate, and what it all means for ordinary Guyanese trying to build wealth in the fastest-growing economy in the world.

item.title: Patriots Portfolio: This Week's Investment Opportunities for True Guyanese
item.description: Your weekly satirical investment advice from the most patriotic portfolio manager in Guyana.
